advantages: Each update uses only a mini-batch, so one epoch costs O(N) work but yields N/B parameter updates—far faster wall-clock progress on large datasets than full-batch descent.
Mini-batch gradients are unbiased estimates of the full gradient, so SGD converges to stationary points under standard assumptions with an appropriately decaying learning rate.
Gradient noise from small batches can help escape poor local minima and flat regions on non-convex losses common in deep learning.
Batched computation maps efficiently to GPU/TPU hardware; moderate batch sizes improve utilization without requiring the entire dataset in memory.
disadvantages: Noisy gradient estimates make the loss curve oscillate; training is less stable than full-batch descent and sensitive to learning rate and batch size.
Very small batches (pure SGD) have high variance, requiring more careful tuning and often slower convergence in terms of epochs compared to larger mini-batches.
The last mini-batch of an epoch may be smaller than B, producing a slightly different gradient scale unless explicitly handled.
Reproducibility depends on random shuffling, data order, and hardware non-determinism—two runs with the same hyperparameters may differ slightly.
